items.title: Random variables
items.description: H(X) is defined from the PMF of a random variable X. You need a valid probability model over outcomes before computing entropy.

items.title: Common distributions
items.description: Bernoulli, categorical, and uniform distributions supply closed-form or standard PMFs used in entropy calculations—binary entropy for Bernoulli(p), log n for discrete uniform on n outcomes.

items.title: Cross-entropy
items.description: Cross-entropy H(P, Q) = −Σ p(x) log q(x) extends H(X) by measuring expected code length under Q when data follow P. For identical P and Q, cross-entropy equals Shannon entropy H(P).

items.title: KL divergence
items.description: KL divergence D_KL(P || Q) = H(P, Q) − H(P) quantifies extra uncertainty when using Q to encode data from P. It is non-negative and zero only when P = Q.

items.title: Loss functions
items.description: Cross-entropy loss in multi-class classification is the empirical average of −log q(y) over training labels—directly tied to entropy and surprise of predicted probabilities.

items.title: Decision trees
items.description: Split criteria based on information gain and Gini impurity both measure how much a feature reduces label uncertainty; information gain is defined explicitly with Shannon entropy.
